headline: Lewis and Clark Camp on Jefferson River, 1805
body:
The morning of July 31, 1805, broke fair and cool, the dew clinging to the wild flax and tansy that carpeted the fertile bottoms of the Jefferson River. Captain Meriwether Lewis, having spent the night alone in this howling wilderness, waited with mounting impatience for the arrival of Captain Clark and the canoes. The river here, a swift and circuitous ribbon of clear water, poured over a bed of smooth pebble and gravel, its banks crowded with small islands and a considerable quantity of cottonwood timber. When the party finally reunited, they broke their fast and pressed onward, their eyes drawn to the lofty, snow-clad peaks of the Tobacco Root Mountains that rose to the southwest, a grand and rugged testament to the earth's ancient upheavals.

As they navigated the rapid currents, the landscape unfolded in a magnificent display of geological wonder. The broad valleys, rich with black loam and teeming with beaver and otter, gradually gave way to narrower passages. High hills of hard black granite and limestone cliffs closed in upon the river, their rocky faces standing like ancient sentinels guarding the secrets of the mountains. "This day proved very warm," one of the men noted; "We continued on our way, and passed clifts of Rocks, and high Walls of Stone lying along the Shores on both sides of the River." The uplands, sterile and composed of light yellow clay, bristled with prickly pear and bearded grass so dry it threatened to burn like tinder at the slightest spark.

The expedition's progress was not without its trials. The men, exhausted from the relentless toil against the current, suffered from tumors, boils, and stone bruises. Sergeant Patrick Gass, having strained his back in a fall, found the labor of the canoes nearly unbearable. Yet, amidst these hardships, the wild beauty of the land offered its own solace. They passed the mouth of a clear, rushing stream that Lewis named River Philosophy, its waters born from the snowy heights and tumbling down through seven mouths into the Jefferson. In the evening, as they made camp on a small island, the hunters wounded a brown bear, though the great beast vanished into the brush, leaving the men without fresh meat for the first time in memory. It was a stark reminder of the wilderness's untamed nature, a place where every step forward was a testament to human endurance against the vast, indifferent majesty of the natural world.
